title: Problem 1 (Albertson)
statement_text: Does there exist a constant $d$ such that whenever $G$ is a planar graph with list assignment $L$ that gives a list of size one or five to each vertex and the distance between any pair of vertices with list of size one is at least $d$, then $G$ is $L$-colorable?
context_text: This problem is posed as motivation for Theorem 5 of the paper, which establishes a related result for lists of size four. It is also connected to Voigt's result that not all planar graphs are 4-choosable. The authors state that building upon the ideas of the paper they give a positive answer to this problem in a separate companion paper [7].
paper_contribution: Theorem 5 proves a related result: every planar graph $G$ with a set $N \subseteq V(G)$ of vertices at mutual distance at least 11 is $L$-colorable for any list assignment $L$ with $|L(v)|=4$ for $v\in N$ and $|L(v)|\geq 5$ for $v\in V(G)\setminus N$. The full positive answer to Albertson's problem is deferred to companion paper [7].
paper_title: 5-choosability of graphs with crossings far apart
